title: Cedar Mailbox Post with Corbels
summary: A cedar mailbox post with decorative corbels, joined with half-laps and biscuits and finished with boiled linseed oil.
description:
A basic mailbox post is a 4x4 in the ground with a box screwed to the top; this one adds a pair of corbels under the mailbox arm, which turns a purely functional post into something that reads as a considered piece of yard furniture.

Cedar is the right species here for two reasons at once: it resists rot without needing pressure treatment, and it's noticeably lighter to set in the ground than a pressure-treated post of the same size.

The finish is **boiled linseed oil, not paint or stain.** Oil soaks into the cedar rather than sitting on top of it, which lets the wood weather naturally over the years instead of needing a repaint when a film finish eventually cracks or peels outdoors.

tools.note: Cuts the half-lap joints where posts and corbels meet.
Routs the 1/2in cove profile on the corbel edges.

materials.name: Cedar, 4x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Main post — allow extra length to cut past any drying checks.

materials.name: Mineral spirits
materials.note: Thins the linseed oil for finishing.

cutList.note: Cut extra length past any drying checks at the ends.

steps.title: Cut the post and corbel blanks
steps.body: Cut the 4x4 post to length, leaving extra at the ends to trim past any drying checks. Cut the corbel blanks from the shorter 4x4.

steps.title: Cut the half-lap joints
steps.body: Lay out and cut half-lap joints where the post and corbels join, checking the fit before gluing anything.

steps.title: Rout the corbel profile
steps.body: Rout a 1/2in radius cove profile along the decorative edges of each corbel.

steps.title: Glue and reinforce the joints
steps.body: Glue the half-lap joints with Titebond III and reinforce each with biscuits, clamping until cured.

steps.title: Assemble the post and mount
steps.body: Assemble the post, corbels, and mailbox mounting arm with deck screws.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Finish with boiled linseed oil thinned with mineral spirits, applied in thin coats so it soaks in rather than sitting on the surface.
